commit.message:
Allow the game to generate Depths:3 again

ed5ad33 moved the Abyss entrance away from Depths:3 and the builder is
asserting that the level is invalid because it can't find an Abyss entrance
there.

It's not great to just *never* check for a required feature in level
validation anymore, but this seems much more complicated now that the
portal is randomly on one of 3 floors (and its placement handled via
serial vault). check_map_validity() operates on a single floor and can't
easily tell what may or may not be on some other floor.

So, while I'd like some better future solution to map validation here,
let's at least not softlock all players in the meantime.
Silence a warning in non-tiles builds

Brief disable orbs of mesmerism on changing floor

While the orb of mesmerism's effect seems reasonably balanced in most other
circumstances, it is overly effective in stair-dancing scenarions. A
guaranteed AoE daze upon entry allows the player to arrive safely, hit
exactly one monster to snap them out of their daze, and pull them back
across the stairs with them - no matter what amount of threat is involved.
It is simply too consistent at creating 1v1s in this scenario, with little
risk involved.

Now, the orb briefly goes on cooldown whenever you take a stair, removing
the most degenerate case.

I've hesitated on this change for a while, in search of a more clean
solution. But every other idea I've been able to come up with (eg: making
daze not block monsters from taking stairs or making the daze effect itself
much less reliable) feels even worse. And since the orb doesn't seem
problematic outside of this specific issue, I'm hoping this will be
a tolerable band-aid.

Heal lurker monsters when they return to lurking

(Since no state changes will happen *while* they're lurking, meaning
injured monsters would never heal.)
Fix antique champions not being affected by LRD (mrmagolor)

Relax Polar Vortex's range check

It was prompting for targets not being in range if they were more than 5
tiles away when you cast it. Which is indeed the maximum range of the
spell, but the damage over time nature of it means that enemies at range 7
are extremely likely to be inside it by the time it even reaches range 5,
anyway. Let's not require a force cast if there's at least something in
LoS.

Give Goji's unmounted form more logical stats

This was effectively a dummy monster, but now that it's possible to get him
as a zombie, he probably shouldn't fly and have fast movement.

Fix some oddnesses with zombifying mounted monsters

When killing a mounted monster with effects like Death Channel, you could
produce two separate spectrals - one for the mount and another for the
mount+rider combo (ie: a goblin rider could turn into a spectral wyvern
and a spectral goblin rider, somehow duplicating the wyvern.)

You were also seemingly unable to get a two different zombies, but either
the mount (rarely) or the merged monster. Even wierder, as a zombie, those
goblin riders would look like goblins, yet fly and move fast.

Now, derived undead effects should be able to make undead out of each
half of the monster separately, but not both together. And yes, you can
get two zombies out of one at once.

Rename the deep elf pyromancer/zephyrmancer enums

Was mildly confusing that they were still FIRE_MAGE and AIR_MAGE

Remove some needless code from god_speaks

The fake monster (used only as a dummy to let do_mon_str_replacements()
work) always counted as visible to the player, which had some very minor
effects (eg: causing it to query the name of the monster and search for
some string replacements that would never be used).

Also, we were resetting it and saving/restoring part of the mgrid every
time we called god_speaks, which seems pointless since it never touches
the mgrid in the first place.
Silence an unused variable warning

Compute somewhat less when getting names of known-invisible monsters

Patterened vaguely after the structure done for regular monsters (ie: don't
bother computing AC, EV, other stats when merely fetching a name).

We're slightly inconsistent about ordering a lot of this and it's still
sort of a mess.
Fixup invis name fix

Make monster displacement from casting Invisibility respect traversability

In other words, rather than a true blink, it has to be somewhere they could
walk to right now (respecting the presence of creatures). Most
consequentially, this means orc wizards can't invis themselves to the other
side of you in a corridors (but also not across rivers, etc.)

(It feels maybe a *bit* weird to have a blink-yet-not-quite, but I might
be happier with the effects of this one.)

Make Goji slightly more consistent about falling off their moth

Any damage taken that lowers them to 40% of their max HP or below now
always kills them (leaving the moth alive), instead of them sometimes
limping along to the very end.

Attribute deaths from Goji's ghost moth to Goji

For stat tracking.

Fix known-invis monsters getting 'invisible' appended to their kill source

eg: deaths by a known-invisible Sigmund would be attributed to 'the
invisible Sigmund'.

And while it's genuinely cute to see quantitative numbers for how much more
dangerous a unique is when they cannot be seen, it generally plays a lot
worse with Sequell.

(I am not 100% comfortable with the code solution to this, which has a
wider number of effects than just scorefile entries, but hopefully it is
fine in all the other cases, too.)

Fix specially-named monsters getting the wrong names while invisible

eg: known-invisible Pan lords would result in lines like
"The invisible  hits you.".

Adjust Abyss rune placement behavior

be59399 intended to lower the rate at which players can find the Abyssal
rune at depth 4 and 5, but accidentally (due to an off-by-one error) made
the chance of placing a rune any time the Abyss generated a new area 100%!
Whoops.... (And also increased the rate on Abyss:3 by 50%, for good
measure.)

However, after more time has passed, I believe that even dialing it back to
the numbers I originally intended would still leave the chances of the
player entering the Abyss virtually on top of the rune much too high.

So now, the player is required to explore a certain amount of the Abyss
before the rune has any chance to place. This is based on the number of
times the Abyss generates new areas, rather than tiles explored (the latter
can vary a *lot* with how open the map randomly is, and I don't want to
penalize avoiding open areas).

Teleport from ?tele scrolls will always move the player 'backwards' (ie:
reduce the number of Abyss areas they count as having explored), though
other forms of teleport (including the Abyss itself pulling you around)
will not. (This is to keep it from being unintuitively a good idea to
immediately burn a couple ?tele scrolls to skip most of the exploration).

The rate of generating the rune *after* minimal exploration is complete is
still meaningfully faster than it was in 0.33, for the record.

Move the Abyss entrance from Depths to D:13-15 in a new serial vault

This dungeon restructuring has quite the multitude of purposes:

 * The Abyss has always been a plausible third rune in a regular game
   compared to the Hells and Pan, but the reliable entrances being placed
   in Depths gives off the impression it's a comparable choice of place
   thus meant for extended wins. While it's always been a nice part of
   Depths's loose identity to have multiple entrances to multiple differing
   differing unholy realms, it's going to continue to conspicuously stand
   out versus the Hell-Pan roulette plans emphasizing the two of those
   as more equal. Moving the Abyss to the floors one can also find the
   Vaults entrance both makes it seem like a more viable third rune option,
   reduces future jarring branch difficulty comparisons, and allows further
   honing in on Depths have a more coherent flavour identity
   after-the-fact.

 * The Abyss entry vaults themselves have been fairly unnoticeable outside
   of the box of outclassed abominations and unseen horrors or some now
   fairly lost Elf:3 deep elves, due to a historical lack of much
   attention for the vault set plus the limited number of Abyss denizens
   appropriate in threat level and on non-Abyss floors. Moving the
   entrance earlier allows more weaker monsters that can show up in the
   Abyss instead of just hurling around high-tier Abyss standout monsters
   like starcursed masses.

 * D:12-15 is a somewhat bland place after Lair, with a few branch
   entry vaults being the only guarantees of any vaults of interest in
   a level range after the branch ends of both Lair and Orc, and the
   Depths entry vault in particularly being fairly restrained by default.
   The Abyss entry vault itself can be another mild highlight that isn't
   just the same parades of bones and tengu and slime, especially with
   a more noticeable variation on this being done here.

 * After the combination of 1e4f93a scaling banishment to the player's XL
   (further updated in 99aa80d) and 00d325f forbidding wielding distortion
   weapons while worshipping good gods, it is now impossible for such
   worshippers to use such unwields to try and time reaching the Abyss:3
   rune depth at the minimal XLs to reach Abyss:3 and not Abyss:4.
   (This is very roughly around XL 17). Timing this sort of thing is going
   to be an inevitable issue slowly increasing over time as people
   consider it a more viable rune, so we might as well also make this
   something that doesn't rely on specific spoilery strategizing and just
   allow manually entering the Abyss earlier anyway.

 * Some people wanting to try out Lugonu have to get fairly lucky to get
   them any passably early in the game, due to the only guaranteed
   entrances being in Depths, though the entire flavour of Lugonu means
   we also don't want to give them any Temple or overflow altar
   arrangement. With future Lugonu changes being planned to potentially
   make them a reasonably enticing planned god for a character even
   outside of exiting the Abyss, being able to go for the god with the
   caveat of the risk of entering the Abyss to do so is a reasonable
   answer to such desires and to support future overhauls.

As such, this commit moves the guaranteed Abyss entries each game from
randomly on each floor of Depths to a serial vault on D:13-15- such a
change is meant to inherit the old identity of the entries appearing
multiple times across Depths in a new unique identity of visibly
"corrupting" the level with multiple entrance vaults instead.

This serial vault comes with new vaults nearly tripling the vault count
to enable three categories of monster density ("empty", "sparse", and
"threat" internally), and then places three entries in total: either 1 of
each, or two empty entries and one fully threatening one. These vaults
mostly use monsters shared in both late D and the Abyss, with scattered
exceptions of brain worms, small abominations, raiju, ynoxinuls, and
(most frequently) abyssal acolytes, all of which are reasonably
appropriate for the depth. (With new threat levels established, this also
means nerfing the previous Abyss entries containing many large
abominations, or liches, or Elf:3 deep elves). Said vaults have some mildly
further degrees of decoration than previously, and regularly emphasize
weird vault connectivity and integration tricks that should hopefully
stand out further, but also be fairly weaker than many of the nasty vaults
that can use many 9s and 8s in D:13-15 already. Hopefully, this should
reasonably work out.

(This also comes with specifying Abyss entrance locations in the four
encompass vaults that can currently rarely place on D:15 as the Depths
entrance, and save compat written up to basically just place a plain
random Abyss entrance on Depths:3 if a save finished D but didn't generate
Depths yet. This commit also doesn't move the Abyss entrances out of
vaults earlier or later, as such entrances have been already scattered
across vaults across the game to begin with.)

Further tweak the Abyss monsters pool

 * With endgame players no longer ever going through Abyss:1 as of
   1e4f93a, and with the ever-increasing interest in making the different
   Abyss depths feel more distinct from each other, I am cutting a variety
   of top-tier endgame threats from spawning on Abyss:1. There's not that
   much of a meaningful difference between "unthinkable to fight" and
   "especially unthinkable to fight", it helps focus the identity of the
   Abyss more on its growing native monster pool, and it makes earlygame
   banishment feel slightly less absurd (+ more capable of trying to
   escape via killing monsters) while still threatening plenty with the
   rest of encounter possibilities.

   As such, one can no longer encounter bone or quicksilver dragons,
   ancient or dread liches, classed draconians, or hell sentinels on
   Abyss:1 outside of Abyss vaults.. They still spawn naturally on
   Abyss:2-7, of course.

 * On the other side of matters, a few more monsters have been removed
   from natural generation in Abyss:5- (jellies, fire bats) and Abyss:7
   (wraiths, shadowghasts, plain vampires, hell hounds), which also
   makes them less common on the floors between those and Abyss:1. While
   weaker undead and elemental fodder are nice for earlier appearances
   having more than bespoke monsters or demons, the breadth of the Abyss
   spawns pool is already large enough without emphasizing monsters
   well past their threat level, and it continues to help discourage
   any (silly notion of ) Abyss farming in 3-rune games.

 * After their buff in 72556b3, eyes of devastation are now reasonable
   monsters to appear on Abyss:7, so they now can spawn there too.

Lightly distribute heralds and acolytes in the Abyss

This was split from the previous commits, due to the fact that heralds of
the Abyss can get abyssal acolytes as band members as they start spawning
at Abyss rune depth. Both are meant to be mildly uncommon versus the
entire breadth of Abyss spawns, and both get bands to emphasize their
organized worship as well as their threat levels.

 * In regular random generation, heralds of the Abyss have a 75% chance of
   getting a band of two other monsters, making it harder to flee the
   tentacle and giving their fights more distractions. The first support
   choice has a 50% chance of being an abyssal acolyte and otherwise
   chooses between rakshasas, cognitogaunts, or worldbinders, and the
   second fodder monster has a nearly equal chance to pull a large
   abomination, golden eye, or thrashing horror.

 * In regular random generation, abyssal acolytes always come with a band
   of one or two other monsters, which include a ~41.6% chance for a
   second abyssal acolyte and otherwise mostly small abominations, with
   fairly low chances each for efreets, freezing wraiths, and ugly things-
   all meant as relatively plainer and weaker monsters to give the
   ever-terrifying Touch of Paradox buff to.

 * Random generation wise, the weight adjustments to accomodate both
   includes scraping some small amounts of weight from monsters already
   plenty prominent in other parts of the game. (It also includes cutting
   bunyips from the Abyss list, as the effort to put at least one monster
   from every branch already is covered by the more interesting
   will-o-the-wisps.)

 * Vault-wise, only a small number of generically-themed Abyss vaults
   have either monster added, which most notably for rune vaults includes
   seething_liches (getting a nerf to the lich choices simultaneously),
   zappy_water_cross (which needs more Abyss linkage and a mild buff),
   and elephants_shrine (which also needs a buff).

While these have some natural other potential uses as was mentioned in
their additioning commits, they are also split off from this commit
due to some additional work needed for the either of them.

New monster: abyssal acolytes (distinct from mad acolytes)

With a non-unique high priest of Lugonu getting canonized in the previous
commit, the rather lower-tier word of "acolyte" (c.f. burial acolytes in
dac2563) is spared up for a lower-tier priest of Lugonu. These have
multiple intents beyond that: they'll provide another monster heavily
themed around Translocations effects as we've got a whole spell school
for, they provide another raw support monster that can work all game long
without being based on debuffs, and they provide another strongly
Abyss-themed monster that can function fine outside of the Abyss.

These abyssal acolytes are relatively weak priests of Lugonu in terms of
their own stats, mildly stronger than orc high priests at most. (They're
also visually further experiments in Abyss's horror / space themes in
being constantly distorted, as a tie-in to both their abilities and to
give the Abyss more things it does beyond ravenous mutating devourers.)
They have relatively plain dagger / whip / athame weapons, and a pair of
notable buffs:

 * They have a new Touch of Paradox spell, which grants a brief dozen-ish
   turns of the buff from the Bane of Paradox- a 25% chance to cast
   Manifold Assault as a natural ability with a 33% afterwards of blinking.
   This serves as another novel way to have a monster buff attacks beyond
   our frequent re-use of might or haste, slowly continues experimenting
   with giving monsters spells as a mechanic that can be deployed in the
   future, and has reasonable texture both midcombat (which threat is
   worth killing first) and between builds (threatening EV defenses and
   allies more than heavy AC melee).

 * They also have the newly-reintroduced Phase Shift from d12a6f1, which
   gives 15 EV and hides monster health bars as long as one lacks See
   Invisible. This of course, gets least one Translocations-themed monster
   actually using the new Translocations-themed effect in a fashion
   helping them survive long enough to use their other buff spell (unless
   people actually actively bother with See Invis). It also pairs abyssal
   acolytes with ogre mages in teaching about the new expansion of See
   Invisible into Phase Sight before it starts getting used in aggressive
   numbers in Elf and Zot.

These will appear throughout the Abyss in numbers higher than the many
random monsters but fairly lower than most other Abyss-exclusive enemies,
at rates similiar to lurking horrors and apocalypse crabs, in the
following commit. (They're probably functional all game long as support
enemy types, but mostly should be focused into Pan and Zot themewise
more than anywhere else.) Quite noticeably, they also will see some late
D usage with some Abyss entrance changes further into this commit batch.

Their tile combines an old orc sorcerer tile and the current Blink icon
together, both by roctavian.

Revise mad acolytes of Lugonu into heralds of the Abyss

1e0e9fc added nearly-unique vault monsters fifteen years ago to guard a
rune vault, a very strange pairing of design intents that hasn't changed
despite many changes to the Abyss otherwise. These "mad acolytes of
Lugonu" were reskinned wizards (later reskinned occultists) with a unique
spell set of Malign Gateway, Smiting, Blink, Blink Other, Invisibility.
While it's nice to keep Malign Gateway rarer due to mostly just sharing
the spell with Mnoleg, it's also weird to still keep around rare vault
monsters in a place so important as a rune vault (even if the Abyss rune
vaults are wildly varying in theme and contents), and we can do with both
more Abyss:7 denizens as well as something that can embody the Abyss while
working outside it for some future vault plans. (Additionally, actually
making them count as chaotic mutants will help with future Lugonu plans.)

These new "heralds of the Abyss" (yellow `ψ`s in console) are somewhat
melodramatically named, but attempt to live up to such with fairly higher
defenses than previously alongside high cast rates. To match their
tentacle-faced tile and indicate they've ascended past being just a random
humanoid, they're now innately chaotic and weak to silver, and also
mutated up a suite of single pips of resistance to fire, cold, and
electricity. While they lose nearly all of their former invocations
(Invisibility, Smiting, Blink, Blink Other) as somewhat generic spells
that many monsters get (and which also feel weakly linked to Lugonu
otherwise), they get to keep some things and get one new trick:

 * They still keep Malign Gateway, as works much better in multiples with
   19424be permitting such, and which still functions fine in or out
   of the Abyss for forcing a certain amount of repositioning away and
   letting more monsters spawn while waiting them out. (Sometimes, they
   might even get lucky with foolhardy or trapped players being unable
   to escape!)

 * They also gain reasonably high cast rates for Warp Space, the
   AC-respecting irresistable-damage 3x3 explosion spell that has a chance
   to blink its victims used by wands of warping. This is fairly nasty-
   their HD makes it deal 3d20- but can help escape as much as it hinders
   such, can't be cast in melee range or with other monsters around, and
   it smoothly compresses together their previous damage and relocation
   spells into one memorably mean effect.

 * Their weapons have been somewhat upgraded- athames, quarterstaves,
   and demon blades / whips- and while in the Abyss they still mostly get
   to keep their distortion-branded weapons. We don't really use much
   distortion in the Abyss itself as drags the player deeper outside of
   spatial maelstroms, but the fear of such should let them continue to
   stand out as high-tier threats. 80% of their appearances in the Abyss
   and all of their appearances outside of the Abyss instead use a mix of
   chaos, draining, and fairly rare weapon-type-specific brands instead,
   as their mechanics are plenty usable elsewhere without threatening
   banishment itself, and to get in a little more direct chaos for one of
   our chaos gods.

This commit only replaces mad acolytes of Lugonu in the two vaults
currently using them, as they are meant to get bands with a monster added
in a following commit, but they will only spawn starting from Abyss:3
and scale slowly to Abyss depth. There are some plans to use these in
god-themed guaranteed demonic rune vaults with Pan changes partially slated
for this version, and they'd be perfectly fine in Abyss-themed Zot vaults
if anybody makes and submits those (and reflecting the branches runes are
kept is a nice angle to continue emphasizing for Zot vaults anyway).

The herald of the Abyss tile mildly modifies the tile Sastreii previously
made for mad acolytes of Lugonu, giving them a new palette and a faint
glow effect derived from Denzi's old jellyfish.

Prevent shouting at inactive clockwork bees (tekkud)

It was slightly odd to be able to order around dormant clockwork bees
when a) they can't move or attack and b) you can't order around active
ones.

Mark tentacles as not following orders

They never really followed orders anyway, because the tentacle behaviour
code ignores the monster's target. This seems fine, because eldritch
tentacles are very eldritch and vines have another master. We make this
official to inform the player and to make it clearer in the code.

Don't allow shouting orders without obedient allies

This could be confusing in the case when the player had non-orderable
allies.

Improve freezing cloud's targeter

This makes freezing clouds targeter and uselessness resist-aware, so it
will no longer autotarget cloud immune or rc-infinity monsters.

We also fix the uselessness check so it no longer leaks the presence of
invisible monsters.

Fixes #5278

Fix breaking a sealed door not restoring its tile

When a monster breaks through a sealed door, we wouldn't end the terrain
change that turned used to seal it (even though it doesn't do anything
anymore) which would result in any tile override that the door had not
being restored.

Fix opening a sealed door not restoring its tile

When a vault warden seals a door, we use a temporary terrain change to
change it into a sealed door. This removes its tile override. However,
if a monster then opens the door, we wouldn't restore its tile override.
Instead, end the temporary terrain change when opening a sealed door
which will restore its tile override.

Fix a crash from attracting summons

We weren't updating player knowledge or the scren after attracting
monsters. This was fine for most cases, because something else updated
the knowledge before our next turn, but in the case of summoned monsters
we could get to the next player turn with incorrect display of monster
positions. After recent changes, this also causes the game to crash in
some cases where we rely on monsterinfo's to match reality.

Stop vault warden tension checks breaking LoS

When we set the door to closed and then open to check tension, we
weren't updating LoS. This sometiems left the LoS cache thinking the
door was closed, e.g. stopping the player from firing through it. I
suspect it could also affect the tension check itself.

This is a consequence of 975d5eb, where we stopped setting the terrain
as changed when opening/closing the door. In the new version, we still
don't do most of the terrain change dance for the reasons stated in that
commit; we just do the LoS invalidation that set_terrain_changed does.
Remove some casts that CAO might be objecting to

Have inner flame place clouds via its explosion instead of separately

While the old method achieved the same effect almost all of the time, if
an inner flame explosion was set off in a wall, it could place clouds on
the other side of that wall, which could sometimes (depending on time
taken and monster speed) damage monsters before automatically dissipating
due to being outside the player's LoS.

Instead, place the clouds via the explosion itself, so they will only
appear on the same set of tiles.

Fix inner flame explosions not working when set off in walls (monkoooky)

If an inner-flamed wall monster died inside a wall, the explosion would
affect nothing at all. This is because explosions can only propogate
through walls if a monster is currently inside that wall (and the wall
monster is already dead by the time the fineff fires).

Tweak a few monster emergency spells

Emergency spells can only be used when a monster is heavily injured, and
those with very low cast rates are very unlikely to be used during that
period of time. This can lead to otherwise-ordinary spells being so
uncommon on some monsters that it's easy to forget they exist at all.

This changes several of these by either removing the spell entirely, or
making it non-emergency, based on whatever felt more appropriate for the
monster.

Merfolk Aquamancers and Revenant Soulmongers can now blink at any time
(though the latter won't use it if already at range), while Hell Knights
lose Blink and Kirke loses Invisibility.

Turn shadow wraiths into lurkers (and buff their stats a little)

They might still be on the weak side for some of their uses, but it's easy
to adjust again.

New unique: Goji, Who Cannot Be Seen.

A particularly intrepid goblin rider who managed to snag a ghost moth as a
mount, firmly believing that invisibility is transitive.

Appears far earlier than ghost moths normally could, but they themselves
are visible (and use draining gaze less often than a normal moth), meaning
they cannot surprise the player. And by the time the ghost moth itself
shows up, it's already wounded and the player knows where it is.

Always spawns wearing a scarf of harm, as some situationally interesting
loot to make them more worth fighting.

(This started as a silly joke, but I really could not resist.)

-------------------------------

Goji's tiles were made by regret-index, combining Denzi's ghost moth with
roctavian and Sastreii's goblin tile and roctavian's edits of ontoclasm's
spriggan rider tile.

Make the monster Invisibility spell include a short-range blink

Monsters now blink to a random location within 2 spaces of their initial
position when they cast it. The idea is to introduce some short-term
ambiguity to the invisible monster's location rather than frequently
leaving it completely obvious where the invisible monster is located.

There are, of course, many more ways now to have the interface reveal their
new location, but it seems like the effect might be more compelling if
there was at least a small moment where the player was unsure where a
monster was.

I am mildly concerned that this is a buff to one of the stronger earlygame
monsters, but hopefully it isn't terribly much in practice (and maybe even
outweighed by the degree to which the new invis UI helps the player
locate and remember where things are). We'll see.

Add a status icon for invisibility on monsters you can see

While this status doesn't do anything to a player who can see invisible
*most* of the time, it periodically results in confusing situations when
the players allies refuse to attack a seemingly-visible monster. This will
hopefully increase clarity in those situations.

(Icon made made by regret-index, modifying the armour of light ego icon
Darby made by modifying ontoclasm's invisibility icon.)
Add a couple things missing from timeout_enchantments()

Replace some monster invisibility with Phase Shift

Phase Shift is an attempt to capture some of the mechanical effects and
benefits of invisibility without the full 'player doesn't know where they
are' part (especially now that the Invisibility spell creates a bit more
ambiguity than it used to).

Monsters enchanted with Phase Shift have +15 EV and their health bar and
state of damage is hidden (which feels like a pretty impactful part of
Invis itself, when it comes to combat assessment). Having see invis negates
these benefits, just as it would for invis itself.

It currently replaces Invisibility on deep elf knights, draconian knights,
ogre mages, and Erolcha.

-------------------------

Bespoke phase shifted tiles were made by regret-index, modifying the
original deep elf knight and draconian knight tiles by roctavian, as well
as roctavian's edits of ontoclasm's ogre mage and Erolcha tiles. The
spell icon reuses the old Phase Shift icon made by roctavian, and the
fallback status icon for phase shifted monsters without special tiles
modifies the armour of light ego icon Darby made by modifying ontoclasm's
invisibility icon.

More comprehensively describe the effects of invisibility to the player

Neither of the means of turning the player invisible described the benefits
of being invisible (and many new players assume it makes them considerably
more immune to enemies, while not realizing its offensive benefits.)

Also do this for monster invisibility (where the major EV and SH penalties
you suffer against the attacks of invisible monsters is most of the reason
that fighting them is dangerous, but is not described *anywhere* that I can
find.)

Remove some pointless code

This value isn't used anywhere I can see or think of. I'm pretty sure it's
a leftover from an earlier version of this spell which never hit trunk.

Remove some redundant code

All of this should be fully vetoed by the targeter already these days.

Don't make Orb of Mayhem's effect depend on the victim's sight

If another monster was invisible to a monster you killed while wielding
orb of mayhem, it would be ineligable to become frenzied. In fact, if you
killed a blind monster, all other targets would be ineligable (since it
can't see *anything* in that state).

Make Transference require a visible target and refactor

In keeping with how virtually all other debuff/utility spells and abilities
that directly target a single monster require the player be able to see it,
prevent using Transference on empty space to 'guess' at an invisible
monster. (I wouldn't be surprised if nobody ever used it this way anyway
once, to be honest.)

At the same time, refactor a bunch of the code to put more of the target
checks and vetoes into the targeter (so it can hilight valid and invalid
targets better) and mark the ability as useless while your ancestor isn't
around.

(Also fix a minor bug where the game thought your ancestor could swap place
with a wall monster, though this merely result in wasting the invocation to
no effect, rather than anything more sinister.)

Add support for 'lurker' monsters

This is a new monster property that is both an attempt to capture some
elements of monster perma-invisibility (eg: surprising the player by
reaching attack range without being spotted) and some aspects of old
monster lurking, with (hopefully) far fewer of its technical burden and
design issues.

Lurker monsters simulate 'hiding' at a position on the map and springing
out in ambush when the player gets nearby. Unlike submerged monsters, they
aren't 'really' on the map and cannot be revealed by blind-firing at random
tiles. Instead, the spot they occupy is internally marked, and whenever the
player reveals it (or makes noise that reaches that spot), the lurker is
put into a queue to spawn near the player after a short length of time (and
possibly only if some other monsters are around).

Once it spawns visibly, it is a normal monster in all regards - fully
visible and without unusual combat behaviors. Although if it loses track
of the player and returns to wandering, it may resubmerge on some nearby
space.

My hope is that the combination of delayed-spawning *and* often preferring
to wait until some other monsters are around before spawning will make most
tedious player behavior to reveal lurkers impractical. There's no avoiding
getting near a specific tile or waiting after each step to see if a lurker
was revealed (you'd hit the Zot clock first). But might still allow the
potentially interesting behavior of 'surprise' appearances by monsters from
short range.

No monsters yet use this behavior, but a few will be added in subsequent
commits.

Slightly simplify exp display for wizmode monster list command

For some reason, it was tallying xp for summons and unrewarding monsters
into its normal xp count and then offering a separate and unexplained
'adjusted' xp that didn't include xp from things that don't give xp.

It's very unclear to me what benefit it serves to list xp from monsters
that can't give xp, so now simply don't.

Allow casting Magnavolt on invisible creatures

The debuff applied by this spell itself reveals invisible creatures, which
was mainly added to avoid the targeter lying to the player if a marked
target went invisible after the fact (which could cause you to fire a
lightning bolt in an unexpected direction).

However, some players found it strange that a spell which reveals invisible
creatures could not be used to actively reveal invisible creatures.

I waffled back and forth on this one for a while, and I'm not entirely
happy with this new state either, but it does seem more consistent with
other spells which can reveal invisible things.

Sadly, if you aim at a truly empty space, the spell whiffs entirely
(without even zapping previously-marked targets) to avoid making it
strictly-optimal to aim at empty spaces after marking all visible targets
'just in case'. (This would almost certainly never be worth doing 99.99% of
the time, but I hope to avoid some outliers from driving themselves crazy.)

This closes #5071 and closes #5086

Make the constriction status of invisible monsters known information

We only display things like BVC or Grasping Roots as a debuff on the
constricted monster, but they are clearly being grabbed by a visible thing
(ie: a swarm of zombie hands) whose status we already get messages about.

To make things a little more clear, constriction status is now shown on
known-invisible monsters and successful constriction will sense them (think
of it as the player inferring their presence and shape by seeing something
grabbing them tightly).

Slightly tweak constriction messages (mostly for monsters)

The messages for ranged constriction (eg: BVC) expiring on monsters was
confusingly similar to the message for a monster breaking free under its
own power. It should now hopefully be a little more clear which is which
(along with a few other tiny wording tweaks).

Slightly increase clarity of Vhi's failure messages

"There's one issue or another keeping you from charging at any nearby foe."
is a famously confusing message to give the player when trying to cast this
spell. While there can be too many targets which are invalid for too many
different reasons to exhaustively list everything, this commit attempts to
exclude the most 'obviously invalid' targets earlier (so that adjacent
creatures don't block messages about more obscure failure states involving
stationary obstructions or mesmerisation). It also reorders a few messages
so that more obvious problems are listed first (and known-invisible
monsters are treated differently than unknown invisible ones).

And in cases where there really are a multitude of equal priority problems
to report, it gives a message that hopefully is still a little less
ambiguous than the old one (which - to me, at least - sounds like even the
game isn't sure what the problem is).

Only hatch seismosaur eggs when non-invisible monsters are near it

Rather than depending on the parent's sight (in other words, just because
you can see an invisible monster doesn't mean the egg will react to it.)

The seismosaur can't see invisible creatures anyway and wouldn't even stomp
on them after hatching.

Make most random-target spells choose only between visible targets

Some spells like Scorch or Permafrost Eruption, which select a random
target from among eligible enemies, currently do not depend on the player's
vision. This means that targeter UI may present a completely different list
of possible targets than the real ones, when invisible foes are involved.
But also that they can unerringly track an invisible monster, no matter
where it moves.

Given current plans to make positional fuzzing of monster invisibility a
bit more relevant, I feel that these spells' ability to ignore all that is
a little too flattening. So as a general policy (agreed to by several other
devs), I am making all 'choose a random target' effects respect the
player's own vision.

Note: multiple spells and abilities already did this (eg: Fulsome Fusilade
and Mark of Carnage summons), and MCC was additionally odd by requiring a
visible target to begin casting, but then being able to hit invisible
targets afterward.

Rework monster invisibility UI

Monster invisibility can create some interesting and memorable situations,
but is somewhat divisive among experienced players. One recurring
complaint is that even in situations where there is no ambiguity about a
monster's identity or location, the interface still makes it more
difficult to deal with them - autofight stops working, many spells require
force-casting (since the game thinks no enemies are in range), and
sometimes the game doesn't do a very good job of making it clear to the
player that there even *are* invisible monsters around if a situation is
crowded enough. Perma-invis monsters can also present learnability issues
for new players, since there is no way to examine them (or even know what
type of monster to look up).

This is an attempt to make the UI work more smoothly around invisible
monsters, and keep better track of known information. In doing so, it
changes a few rules around what counts as 'known information' regarding
invisible monsters. It is my hope that these changes allow preserving some
of the interesting situations invisibility can create while reducing
unwanted friction.

First of all, the old 'outline of a sensed invisible monster' system is
completely replaced. Instead, monsters whose positions are unambiguously
known to the player are drawn using their normal monster tile (somewhat
transparently, and with an additional icon) and most parts of the UI
regarding examining and targeting them should now treat them similarly to
a visible monster.

To be clear, these monsters are *still invisible*; the player cannot
obtain any information about their health or current status effects, still
suffer combat penalties against them, and cannot target them with effects
that strictly require a visible target. This is purely a UI convenience
for the many situations where the player could already deduce this
information.

These 'known invisible' monsters can be sensed via most methods which
previously created monster outlines (ie: by being in shallow water or
clouds, or firing spell projectiles), but also several others ways, such
as 'bumping into them'. In other words, walking into a ghost moth will
reveal specifically that there is a ghost moth on that tile and display it
as such. This means that an invisible monster's type is now considered
public information in any scenario where a monster has been sensed. (Doing
so greatly simplifies the interface regarding them, allows people to xv
things like unseen horrors, and I think comes with little cost since the
number of situations where an invisible monster could realistically be
more than one type of thing is very small.)

On top of this, invisible monsters whose position was *recently* known,
but have since moved, are drawn with a variation on this tile to indicate
they are no longer there (but likely still nearby). This should help the
player keep track of what threats are still around, even if they cannot
currently see them. As such, 'whether a known-invisible monster has moved'
is also now considered public information. Assume that the player is using
hints like the sound of footsteps or changes in airflow to infer, in a
general sense, that movement has occurred.

Finally, in cases where a monster has performed some action that informs
the player of their presence *without* revealing their location in any way
(most commonly when a perma-invis monster attacks the player in melee),
the game will still add them to the monster list and treat them for a
short while as if they were nearby, for purposes of delays and
autoexplore. (Fully sensing a monster just from being hit by it would
significantly defang the interesting positional play that can come from
ambushes by ghost moths and unseen horrors, so you still have to actually
interact with them successfully *yourself*).

This new system of treating invisible things which are *probably* nearby
as threats (for autoexplore/delays) fully replaces the old autopickup
autotoggle. It works more reliably, in my experience, than the old system
did (which, for example, failed to interrupt travel while being attacked
by invisible things, so long as they *missed*, and would turn back on at
the first invisible thing killed, even when multiple were known to be
around.) and also doesn't let you forget to turn it back on (and
periodically cause newcomers to be confused as to why they stopped picking
up things).

A few caveats here:

When a 'remembered invisible' monster is nearby,
spell_no_hostile_in_range() will treat them as potentially always being in
range (so as to avoid the needless friction of needing to force-cast in
scenarios where there is almost certainly a hostile nearby). This *does
not* properly account for the monster's resists, entirely because the code
involved made this very awkward. I hope this will be a very minor issue in
practice.

The blocking of autoexplore uses a LoS check to the position the player
was standing in when the monster was last sensed. In my experience, this
is 'correct' the vast majority of the time (in that it blocks autoexplore
when an experienced player would realistically consider it unsafe, and
doesn't otherwise), but there are some very specific situations where the
player can know they're safe nonetheless (an obvious example involves
using Imprison to put walls in the way of the general area where an
invisible monster was known to be). In order to prevent the player from
simply being unable to autoexplore here, I've added a new command to
temporarily 'forget' about nearby invisible threat (CMD_IGNORE_INVISIBLE,
bound to Ctrl+T by default). This is a bit clunky, but I was unable to
devise a heuristic that didn't have *some* fail cases, and I believe the
player should almost never have to use this command.

No *all* actions an invisible monster can perform cause the player to
sense them. While beam code covers many spells, there are many, many
spells that do not use it. For instance, an invisible monster can summon
creatures in LoS of the player without this giving away their presence
(even though the player can tell that something *must* be nearby).
However, I feel strongly that full coverage is *not a pragmatic goal*, nor
worth the code complexity. Turning invisible while in LoS already senses a
monster, so they would need to first turn invisible while out of LoS
(itself a rarity), and even then, the summons themselves will interrupt
delays (and it's unlikely the monster will not be sensed in some other way
while the player was fighting them). Antimagic Gaze in particular *does*
alert (since it's used by a common permainvis monster), but I feel that
covering the vast majority of common cases is better than suggesting a
standard whereby every way in which the player could infer a monster's
presence was handled by the UI. That way lies madness.

---------

In terms of code structure, you.aware_of() is a new method that includes
both you.can_see() *and* the presence of known-invisible monsters. For
most UI and targeting purposes, aware_of() is now the method that should
be used.

Since known-invisible and visible monsters now look rather more similar to
each other than before, this batch of commits will also attempt to enforce
some general principles about what does or doesn't work on invisible
monsters:

-All beams, explosions, and projectiles can affect and be aimed at
invisible monsters
-Spells which hit an entire area (or all of LoS) can affect invisible
 monsters
-Spells which automatically target a *random subset* of monsters in LoS
 will *not* target invisible monsters
 -This was true for several of these already, but will be applied more
  universally in a subsequent commit
-Non-damage spells which directly target a single enemy cannot affect
 invisible monsters (unless part of the point is that they can, such as
 Yara's)
 -This was already true of most of these, but will be applied to a few more
  in a subsequent commit

In addition, several player downsides that depended on the presence of
only visible monsters (to avoid leaking information) are now active while
known-invisible monsters are nearby (eg: 'No regen near monsters'). No
more removing your SInv ring to conveniently forget about things.

Finally, the old 1-in-7 chance to passively detect any nearby invisible
creature no longer exists. (I feel like there are plenty of ways to get
information on them that this isn't needed; in many ways, it was working
as a poorer version of the known-invis system, except that it also worked
on sleeping monsters at random sometimes.)

(This is a very large commit that touches enough different things that I
am not confident I've explained everything it does in even this much text,
but hopefully am close.)

Make a few debug messages print real names of invisible monsters

There's no point in hiding information in diagnostic messages.
Don't give fewer decor messages when unseen monsters are nearby

Remove some unused function declarations

Unused since ffccbfb

Fix some item reward access in _gulch_crossways_interlock (Odds)

There's no way to arrange proper full connectivity for the subvault's
secondary corner chambers for the four different door configurations
without adding substantially more doors. As such, this rearranges it to
have three set-ups, with the outer connecting doors getting multiple doors
at a lower chance than rolling either inner door placements.

Don't have monsters pick up two ranged weapons

Monsters who carried a melee and ranged weapon were swapping out the
melee weapon for another ranged one.

Fix a couple of problems with shadow coglin ranged

Shadows were not reliably wielding ranged two weapons; depending on the
order they were checked in, they sometimes ended up with one.

Additionally, when they did have two weapons, they were shooting the
same one twice (and then swapping between attacks).
Support UTF8 characters in clipboard

Handle unicode characters in the monster list pane.

Use unicode-aware functions to calculate the length of a monster
description in the monster list and resize it where needed.

Don't crash if there's somehow only 1 character left for a monster's
name in the monster list. It can't happen at present, but it seems like
an odd ASSERT().

Committer's note: also pulled out a variable for readability.
Checkwhite

Fix Dungeon and Depth wall tiles sometimes using wrong variations

tile_init_flavour should give the same result when called on a tile that
has had its flavour initialize and one that hasn't. However, this wasn't
the case for the way it picked Dungeon and Depth tiles variations. This
problem was very noticable when placing a local vault in wizmode as it
calls tile_init_flavour on every square on the level.

Remove an unneeded call to tile_init_flavour

There was a call to tile_init_flavour in vault_placement::apply_grid
that happened before setting flavour. This call was initialy added to
fix some tiles being completely black in abyss vaults. However, the
abyss sets crawl_state.generating_level to true when placing vaults and
this call only happens when its it to false. Instead the abyss now
handles this in _abyss_fixup_vault.

Fix monsters opening clear sealed doors turning them opaque

If a monster opened or broke a clear door that had been sealed by a
vault warden, you would get a regular open or broken door rather than
the transparent version.

Make gavotte predict harm more accurately

For targetters and penance checks:
* In the case where the monster ends up stopping due to water/lava,
  don't predict harm.
* Predict actor collisions in cases such as flyer-air-non_flyer-lava.
* Predict collisions with monsters out-of-los (which won't move with
  Gavotte)

We do this by simulating the whole gavotte movement with player
knowledge rather than ground truth.

Fixes #5314

Fix some penance edge cases with gavotte

* Do penance even when the victim dies, by moving the penance check
  before damage (this still messages *after* the collision damage).
* Have penance rely on "could you see the monster at the start of the
  turn". This was previously using the player's new position, possibly
  after gavotte movement, and the monster's new position, which led to
  some odd outcomes.
* Last and very much least, give penance for friendly wall walkers
  damaged by an incoming collision from a monster who can't live in
  their wall.
Warn for penance from various non-beam spells

Fix xom scenery changes sometimes not being noticable

When Xom changes scenery, the tile override of the changed squares
wasn't being reset. This would result in the tile not changing for any
squares that had a tile override.

Fix placing a vault in mizmode not marking in view terrain changed

Almost everything that set_terrain_changed does (deleting clouds in
solid features, updating slimey wall tracking, updating mold tracking,
firing terrain changed evens, etc.) still needs to happen for in view
terrain. However, we were excluding calling it for in view terrain
presumably so that the terrain didn't get marked as changed. This isn't
actually a problem though as the terrain changed flag will get removed
when we next call viewwindow anyway and if it actually is a problem, it
would make more sense to fix set_terrain_changed as there are so many
other places that assume it is fine to call it for in view terrain.

Fix _tension_door_closed setting some squares as changed

_tension_door_closed closes a door checks the tension and then restores
the door. However, when closing the door we would mark the square as
change, remove all the clouds in the door, and fire off a terrain
changed even. We were making sure to restore the clouds but the way we
did this was not great for performance. Item and monster detection have
complex interactions with changed terrain and it's unclear if firing off
a terrain chenged even is harmful, so it's probably best not to do this
when the terrain hasn't actually changed.

Make some vaults which rely on stairs transparent

Stairs can occasonally be removed from vaults. If they need
those stairs, it's important to mark them transparent so that
they are included in connectivity checks.

Move pool fixup earlier

Deep vs shallow water affects connectivity, so we should update this
before checking connectivity and closets. We move this into the
post_vault_build function so that it is still used as before by all
game modes.

Create a tag to allow tele closets

Some vaults deliberately place teleportation closets, which
they offer item-based escapes from. Now that we mask such
closets by default, we create a tag to allow this, and apply
it to three existing vaults which intend to provide escapes.

Detect and mask teleport closets during level generation

We have a number of strategies to fix and veto teleport closets
during level generation. They mostly work, but many vaults have either
deterministic or occasional teleport closets built in. Here, we add a
way to explicity search for such vaults. This is far more permissive
than other connectivity checks, allowing traps, transporters, etc as
ways to escape.

Empirically, this mostly finds two class of vault:
* Vaults with a bug, that should have more no-tele squares or some
other fix.
* Vaults which procedurally generate tiles which may get disconnected
in ways that would be hard to avoid. Typical examples are large
expanses of mixed deep and shallow water.

To fix these, we mask found teleportation closets with no_tele_into.
Additionally, to aid debugging of closets, we enable a mapstat
argument which turns these into vetoes.

Support pasting into line input in local tile

This is especially useful for pasting in long vaults in wizmode.

Refactor: use an unwind_var in _cull_redundant_stairs

This makes it clear that we only wish to remove the staircase
temporarily. It also makes restoring the staircase less error prone.

Update the documentation of a parameter of _fixup_stone_stairs

_fixup_stone_stairs no longer turns staircases into hatches

Refactor: rename a confusing parameter of _cull_random_stairs

The replacement feature isn't a hatch anymore

Refactor: rename a confusing parameter of _cull_redundant_stairs

The replacement feature isn't a hatch anymore

Fix _cull_redundant_stairs not clearing flavour from removed stair

If the staircase that got replaced had a tile override, its replacement
would use this tile override making it look like a staricase. This
didn't matter previously as _cull_redundant_stairs was effectively
unused due to an inverted condition.

Fix _cull_redundant_stairs doing nothing

It's check to see if stairs needed culling was inverted which resulted
in it always doing nothing as it was only called when stairs needed
culling.

Fix Gozag shops sometimes looking like walls

Dungeon generation can sometimes generate floors with feat flavour set
to a wall tile. This is fine as feat flavour is ignored on floors.
However, if the player converts one of these floors to a shop using
Gozag, we wouldn't clear feat_idx which is used to restore the feat
flavour after a reload resulting in the shop looking like a wall after a
reload.

Fix the preview of a Ru mutation

Ru upgrades your innate mutations not your normal ones - fix the
mutation preview to account for this.

This could be slightly unclear in the case that the normal mutation
is two levels above the innate one, but this is pretty niche and
certainly better than the current situation where we lie about
increasing the level.

Fixes #3949

Fix angering tentacle segments

This fixes a bug whereby attacking the middle of a friendly eldritch
tentacle caused only that section to appear hostile for one turn,
AFAIC with no mechancial effect. Now tentacles correctly object
to you hitting them anywhere at all.

We do this by generalising get_tentacle_head to work for solo tentacles.
This also fixes the threat assessment of eldritch tentacle segments,
which now inherit from their root like krakens already did.

We fix some callers of get_tentacle_head who were unnecessarily calling
it twice (it was already idempotent, though now this is perhaps more
obvious).

Fixes #2843

Fix ruined levels and vaults not respecting FTILE definitions

After commit c73acac we would clear the flavour from a square when
ruining it to stop, for example, a wall that had been turned into
shallow water from still looking like a wall. However, this resulted in
us clearing the floor flavour (defined by FTILE) even though this still
makes sense for the ruined square. Instead, only clear the feat flavour
as this is the only type of flavour that can cause problems when the
dungeon feature has been changed by ruining the square.

Stop ignition firing through walls

Like the fix to fusillade in 93e8449, ignition was hitting squares
behing walls when hitting wall monsters. Instead, use the standard
machinery to make the explosions flow around walls.

We make an explosion iterator to make that "standard machinery" less
boilerplatey.

Also fix the targetter, which was wrong in a different way (assuming
nothing behind a wall would be affected).

Fix wizmode placed features not using tile variations until reload

The code for creating a dungeon feature in wizmode was setting the
"special" flavour for the square to zero and then relying on
dungeon_terrain_changed calling tile_init_flavour to reinitialize it.
However, after commit 4f1e9c7 dungeon_terrain_changed no longer calls
tile_init_flavour as it no longer needs to call it to handle doors and
calling it would sometimes also cause an info leak.

Fix item method docs

Ldoc was generating item methods with a self param (e.g.
`item:inscribe(param)` notation), when the correct usage is
`item.inscribe(param)`.

Committer's note: extended to a few zero-parameter methods, where
the colon usage actualy works but would fail to do so if there
were any arguments.

Stop fusillade firing through walls

When fusillade hit a wall monster, it hit all the squares around that
wall. We fix this to use the standard machinery to calculate the
affected squares.

Fixes #5282

Prevent teleport closets made of traps

The connectivity checks in dungeon generation treat traps as impassable.
This means that they completely ignore areas made *entirely* of traps.
But the player can teleport into these, so that's not ideal.

We fix this by doing an extra connectivity check, vetoing any areas that
are disconnected from stairs when treating traps as passable.

On a sample run of 400 spider levels (which are by far the largest
offenders because of extreme web density), this causes 26 new vetoes out
of a total of 273 vetoes.

Fixes #5294 (this issue may not look like a region made entirely of
traps, but morally it is - most of the region is a vault, which is
invisible to the connectivity algorithm except for its entrance, which
has a trap.

Fix spurious messages during volcano catchup

When checking whether the player is close enough to hear the volcano,
we need to check whether the player is on the map at all.

Fixes #5290

Suppress an out-of-LoS bolas message

Fixes #5300

Fix some effects being endianness dependent

Some calls to hash32 were passed an array of uint32_t. However, because
hash32 works an an array of bytes, the different byte ordering of
uint32_t between big and small endian platforms would result in it
returning a different value. This mainly only affected graphics such as
the tile used by a ghost or which tile variation was used by a dungeon
feature or the description of a random pandemonium lord. However, it did
affect the seed stability of the "sense surroundings" mutation, although
it is unclear to me if this mutation having seed stability is even
intensional.

Fix messages from portals after excursions

When an excursion was triggered - which can happen in a number of ways
including by viewing another floor - timed portal message were then
being suppressed until the portal timed out.

This was happening because of a fix back in e27d229, which added a guard
to fix a bug with too-early messages using the wrong player coordinates
for a portal message on re-entry. Messages were off when the marker was
initialised, and toggled on when entering the level. But excursions
initialise the marker without firing an entered_level event, leaving
messages toggled off.

We fix this by setting the toggle in init only for initial marker
creation, and thereafter toggling it on level entry and exit.

Fixes #5301

Fix breaking large doors

Monsters weren't breaking down doors if the player was adjacent to the
door but not to the square the monster was opening, leading to infinite
door wars.

Fixes #5309

Committer's note: cosmetic change to any_of, reworded commit.
Track tithing piety
